commentBody: I am not a speed solver. I do not rush. I solve with a stylus on a stone tablet. I should not finish a Saturday this quickly.

"Any large reptiles around these parts?"
"I'll find one sooner or gator."

("If you see his cousin, there'll be crocodile cheers.")
Yikes, with several things I just didn't know I just had to keep guessing before the software conferred its confirmation on me.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
Agree with Barry.  A Saturday solve should not take me fewer than 20 minutes.

And yet, the clueing was downright delectable...dare I say Weintraubian...and made both my hemispheres very happy.
Am I getting smarter? Or are these puzzles getting easier? Couldn't possibly be the former...

21A.

Proof beyond a shadow of a doubt that this is not your father's NYT crossword.

Not that I mind--I think it offers a whole new facet to puzzling.

I have very little interest in entering the conversation about how easy the puzzles have become. But I do have an observation. I sometimes do weekend puzzles from the archives and almost invariably find them easier than the current puzzles. There is a fairly obvious explanation – I am 87 years old. The proper names and contemporary cultural references have become elusive.

I still like them, even if they are not from my generation. And I love it when there is trivia from the 50s and 60s.

SOONERORLATER, we're going to see if this DETENTE with Iran holds, or if one side exclaims WEHADADEAL while the other side WREAKS havoc on the global economy.

We will not be able to RESTEASY until this MESS is all sorted out. For now, we'll just have to HANGTIGHT.

IMFORIT, but the ODDS are not in favor of lasting peace.

From DOG FOOD to CAT FOOD before finally getting PET FOOD. 

Great clue for ROOKIE MISTAKE [Cause of amusement to a vet, maybe]. The misdirect fooled me until I had it almost completely filled in. 

Nice debut of CALIFORNIA ROLL and a good clue too. 

My family always called an ottoman a HASSOCK, so I liked seeing that word in the puzzle. 

An enjoyable solve.

Fastest solve since Wednesday. 13 minutes.

Guess what the sixth most common element in the universe is. And guess what two letters it shares with the fifth most common...

Fun fact: I recently finished my 4 week road trip from Florida to Texas back to New York. The ALTIMA I picked up in West Palm had 1500 miles on it. And had 8500 miles on it when I returned it in New York. The longest part of the 7000 miles was the 10 miles leading up to the Holland Tunnel. And the hardest part of the drive was the 3 miles I drove in Manhattan...
Haha micro or macro - aMFORIT seemed just as reasonable as IMFORIT. took me forever to find my mistake, although yes otherwise it was a very fast solve for a Saturday.
Quick and too easy. Interesting, though. Thanks. 🌙

Safe words in our bedroom:

SPIN OFF!

FEES? WE HAD A DEAL!

SAG, RAMROD!

HANG TIGHT, PARD

DFW ME!

***

A fine fast run of a puzzle, sure to irritate the vets in this crowd who will say it's barely more difficult than a standard Tuesday puzzle. Hassock is a great word, possibly the greatest word ever. All hail hassock!

We live in an interesting universe, full of trends and breaks in trends.

One of the reasons NEON is so abundant is that it has a "perfect" nucleus, meaning it can be thought of as made of alpha particles, also known as helium nuclei.

An alpha particle is two protons and two neutrons.  Helium is extraordinarily stable, both in chemical and nuclear terms.

If I take five alpha particles of 2 protons, 2 neutrons each, we get 10 protons and 10 neutrons, which is the NEON nucleus.

Four alpha particles is 8 protons and 8 neutrons, which is the very common oxygen atom.

And three sets of alpha particles is 6 protons and 6 neutrons, the very common carbon atom.

It works larger, too.  Six alphas is magnesium-24, very common, seven is silicon-28, very common, and eight is argon-32, and ten is (20 proton, 20 neutrons) calcium-40, all quite common.

The weird one is two alpha particles, (4 protons, 4 neutrons).   Beryllium-8 is *not at all stable*.  In fact it is one of the *least* stable nuclei.  If you had a gram of Be-14, in 80 billionths of a billionth of a second you'd have only 1/2 gram of Be-14, the other half gram having decomposed into two alpha particles.

I wish I were a Be-8 nuclei.

I started very slowly on this one until I got to CROCODILETEARS, and then the puzzle opened quickly like a blossom in a time lapsed photographic sequence.  Once you get a long entry the puzzle will seem easy SOONERORLATER, provided you don’t make a ROOKIEMISTAKE.  I thought there were some nice clues, like tools for musketeers for RAMRODS  and blitzed for SAUCED (I knew sauce was a term for booze, but never thought of SAUCED as a term for drunk).    While I do always relish a stern challenge, I’ll leave the verdict as to whether this one was too easy or not to the experts, and just say that I enjoyed it.

Three cheers for the reference to MARTIN Gardner whose math  puzzles and essays provided so much pleasure (and mental stimulation) in my nerdy adolescent years.

This is the first Saturday in a couple weeks I successfully finished.  A couple long clues I like such as ROOKIE MISTAKE, CALIFORNIA ROLL, etc.

A few answers I got entirely through crossings I still don’t understand.  DETENTE crossed with ARLES.  PARD crossed with HASSOCKS.  When I entered that E in DETENTE/ARLES I was a little surprised it finished the puzzle because both those answers seemed wrong.

I liked this puzzle.  At first, I thought I might have to look up a word or two, but I got 'em all.  I like clues such as "Cause of amusement to a vet, maybe" and "Breakfast dish that might go over well?" that get me started in one direction, but I later figure out once I have enough crossing words.  Bravo!

I enjoyed it. Was pretty stumped actually until I got to the bottom but CROCODILETEARS really opened it up.  Can’t complain about the difficulty overall; if I had a negative it would be the lack of punny clues, except maybe FRIED EGGS. But an intriguing grid and a decent challenge for me, so thanks for that!
Liked this one, I just wanted to like it for longer. Too easy for a Saturday.
Fun!  I'm getting better at this... : )

A faster than usual Saturday for me, but I did have to work for it. I got the blowgun projectiles answer immediately, but then it was BAMA, then DFW, which got me 20A and 23A. That process was the way my entire solve went—a little here, a little there, and then some fills happened, and repeat.

Thanks, Jesse

I enjoyed the puzzle as always. Long time reader, first time commenter. I feel compelled to say, “Well, actually….” The only clue I would suggest is just off is the one for 18A, (new outfit or accessory in video-game speak,) which I would answer with COSMETIC. A mod would be something that modifies a base game, and while the modification could be an item or outfit created by someone other than the developer, it is usually more game-changing.

Better clue perhaps: Reddit referee, slangily

Okay, I’ll put down my index finger now.
Good Wednesday puzzle. Fresh and fun.
On the easier side. ~14mins with 0 hints. Still pretty fun.
Unlike all the rest of you, I thought this one was hard! But I finally got it with no help. Also, I find the archives puzzles almost always harder than they are today.
Very sweet puzzle. Every answer brought joy.
Having rolled my eyes at 29A, I appreciated the constructor’s self-deprecating description of it as “gunk”.  Overall, I thought this was a solid effort and I enjoyed it. Thanks, Jesse.

The town of ARLES [11D Backdrop for many van Gogh paintings] produced many backgrounds for Van Gogh, but the HUDSON valley [15A New York's____Valley] was the inspiration for a whole school of painting. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hudson_River_School

Not gonna lie, this puzzle reminded me of the time I met up with Elaine Stritch for coffee at Cafe Reggio in the 80s. We talked about Sondheim, the shifting tastes on Broadway at the time, and the way Bruce Vilanch always seemed to sneak up on you when you lest expected it. We shared some laughs, and a tiramisu, and began to wax a bit nostalgic for the days of the Gershwin and Rogers musicals of the old days. At some point we realized afternoon had turned to dusk, and it was time to pay the bill. That’s when my cappuccino started shaking and bubbling out of nowhere. And then sure enough, as if on cue, out popped Vilanch. Right out of the coffee cup.
Good Saturday. Challenging. My only error was CLE. I wrote it as CLA 🙄. The amount of self frustration is immeasurable.

Well I certainly didn't find that one easy at all. Partly due to all the America-related stuff (which I like learning about, but often blocks the way), and partly due to some nice off-kilter clues.
Good Saturday AFAIC.

Enjoyed this! 
I guessed  CALIFORNIA ROLL which was validated by Orr from Catch 22, which immediately made me think of Vance who always looks like he has crab apples in his cheeks.
Great crossword! Lots of good comments too. The word HASSOCK puts me in mind of an *extremely* rude limerick about the Bishop of Birmingham…
Like solving with training wheels.
Good puzzle. Relatively quick for a Saturday. MOD was the last to fall. But I didn’t get the clue for the answer SAFEWORD… ?

Found it difficult to get started, but once I started filling  in the bottom right and found CROCODILE TEARS things started to fill in nicely.  Had to also make some corrections HoldTIGHT vs HANGTIGHT, caTFOOD to PETFOOD etc.  Definitely was an enjoyable solve!

I was not on the same wavelength as this constructor so after about fifteen minutes I put it aside to return in the morning. When I came back it still did not yield for me and the timer showed 2 hours thirty minutes! How does that happen? It certainly will throw off my averages. A disastrous puzzle for me.

I am not a native Wolverine, but it is a sign of my assimilation that my first thought for 33D [It takes two hands to show] was "Map of Michigan!" Lance and Nina choose a SAFE WORD: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AUqs6OGkUdE&t=2s
Between yesterday and today, two really great challenges. Thanks to all the constructors who make Fri/Sat so much fun.
Too easy for a Saturday.

An interesting mixture of too easy (I slapped down POISON DART without any crosses, FRIED EGGS with only one G, and MELTED? C'mon!), quite tricky (it took me quite a while to get REST EASY and HANG TIGHT, and DOCTOR was oddly hard for me),
But mostly a lot of "Oh, I see.  That's a good one!" moments.  I'm thinking SAFE WORD, of course, but also ROOKIE MISTAKE  and DAMSELS and MALTESE.

My assessment?
Nice puzzle. IMHO, it could've been swapped with yesterday's, which I thought was a good notch trickier.
But nothing much to complain about.
Thanks, Jesse Cohn.

Just the other day, someone posted a picture of one on the Way With Words page I visit on FB, and the responses were divided between "ottoman" and "HASSOCK," though many stated they had never heard of HASSOCK. Love these coincidences.

A staccato of happy pings throughout.  Entertainment in the box today:  
•	It came from words and phrases I love, such as ROOKIE MISTAKE, WREAKS, HANG TIGHT.
•	Also from misdirects and vagueness in cluing, so that when the answer came, it came with a “Hah!”  Clues like [Thawing] for DÉTENTE, [Treat] for DOCTOR, and [Silky-haired toy] for MALTESE.
•	More happiness when I laughed at myself after confidently throwing in ALLIGATOR TEARS.
•	My heart melted when I uncovered HASSOCKS, a word my great-grandma Annie – who I revered – often used.  Then another happy ping from seeing it as HAS SOCKS, and now I can’t un-see it.
•	A wow and a bow at [It takes two hands to show] for TEN, a delightful clue I’d never seen before, and one that made a common answer special.  (A post-solve search confirmed it was indeed original.)

You created more than a puzzle, Jesse.  You created a generous helping of feel-good, and that is a gift.  Thank you!
Clever but too easy for a Saturday. Finished in half my usual time. That’s not a good thing.

I enjoyed this solve *very* much
for each and every minute that I was solving.

That’s a nice thing.

Perhaps a clearer post of the NYTimes 4/19 Sunday Crossword puzzle error rather than a small editors note that I had to search around for is warranted.  Saying an error in the grid is an understatement.  It's completely mismatched and kind of shocking that it was missed given how completely off it is (20 clues missing, across vs downs totally mixed up).  It happens but perhaps a clearer communication out versus finding a reddit post that linked to the small editors note here.

Dragons then Daemons before DAMSELS in the SW corner. Happy that WREAKS didn't have to come out.  

The top half filled in mostly painlessly.  Thanks, Grant for your It’s always BAMA comment the other day. But the SE corner gave me fits.  Assumed the Vet was an animal doctor, thought the Musketeers would have some type of sword, and had Pigslop instead of PETFOOD for a time.  

All in all, a good challenge for me. (Since I still have PTSD from years of not being able to solve Saturday puzzles,  I'm inclined to think that if I solved one, it must be on the easier side.)

Why I immediately thought "stoned" over "sauced," I'll never tell. Perhaps I was anticipating a pre-4/20 reference?
Safe word was a bit edgy (if I were a puritan).

It flowed well, covered a lot of ground, and had an old-timey feel. I liked how it bounced around.
I'm for it.

For paper solvers of the Sunday puzzle, there's an editor's note at the end of today's Wordplay column about how the puzzle in the Magazine is wrong and where to find the print version of the  corrected puzzle.

I really enjoyed this one -- just the right difficulty (for me) for a Saturday with great cluing.

I haven't thought about Martin Gardner in a very long time. My father used to enjoy his "Mathematical Games" column in Scientific American.

This was a very unenjoyable solve. Not overly difficult, but some of the most dry cluing I've ever seen especially with the quotes and unclever "misdirects". Also too much random PPP and sports trivia. 44 and 46A were decent but that's it.
That was a treat. Thank you.
Thank the maker for four sports clues on a Saturday, and at good spots for crossing. RAPIER instead of RAMROD was brutal. Cheers!

For all the “too easy” folks here’s a slightly different view. I preface that I solved it in an average time (just below). 

DWADE is esoteric. MARTIN Gardner is not universally known. I’ve flown in DFW but it is not a no brainer — other airports are available. BAMA is sports trivia for a game only played in the US and not even first league trivia. As CLE would be. Metro was a car in the UK but it was made by Leyland which doesn’t fit. Blitzed doesn’t mean drunk anywhere. If anything it reminds one of the weather or war. 

PARD is not a word, and ‘over well’ only possibly describes 🍳 in the US. 

So for the rest of us where all of these had to be deduced rather than being taken as ‘gimmes’ this was a decent Saturday.

can someone smarter than me explain why, if the constructor wasn't happy with 29A (understandably), they didn't sub the P for a W and use some variation of following clues? 29A: a protector / 29D: alternative to kibble. seems more natural to me...

Maybe this is before my time, but as an avid gamer I have never heard an outfit or accessory for a character referred to as a "mod." A "mod" is a user-created modification to the game itself. A new outfit for a character would be called a "skin," or just an accessory/outfit.

Any puzzle that has a reference to Catch-22 is fine with me.  A bedeviling book, having started it and given up a few times before conquering it.  Little did I know that I would run into some of the same lunacy during my own time in the service.

Although many comments have referred to the puzzle as too easy for a Saturday, it was a “Goldilocks” puzzle for me.  

My initial confusion with the Musketeer clue had to do with the Three Musketeers seemingly spending more time in sword play than in utilizing their namesake weapons.

Unusual week where Thursday took me longer than Friday and Saturday combined. I’m not complaining - I enjoyed all three. I liked the grid and long answers in this one. It took me a surprisingly long time to remember that The Simpsons was a spinoff, and I just saw Tracy Ullman in the HBO Mel Brooks documentary last night. Loved that show. I would say “go home!” but I expect most of you already are.

Both yesterday's puzzle and today's seemed too easy (to me) for their respective days. That said, today's took me almost half-an-hour to solve, despite it having far too many "gimmes" for a Saturday puzzle. I must have been on the wrong "wavelength" today, in addition to feeling strangely brain-fogged. 

Fill like IMFORIT, MADEIT, SOLDTO, INFOR, FONDOF, ILY and ATONERS should have kept this one from publication, at least in the NYT. That said, the long entries were solid and some were interesting.

TIL that NEON is fifth-most abundant element in the universe. That surprised me, and now I find myself wondering why this might be. It's always nice when a puzzle leaves me with a question to pursue, so thanks for that. Maybe I'll find an answer in the comments?

Never been fond of small dogs, so perhaps it's not surprising that my first entry for "Silky-haired toy" was CHIAPET and not MALTESE.

Hey Jesse, I think PARD is a great word (I'm a big fan of old timey westerns).  Excellent job working in so much multiple word fill. That's really hard to do and I had a lot of fun working them out. A+.

I wish they would limit the number of banal phrases occurring in the puzzles, especially Thursday through Sunday. It was really egregious today. 12A, 27A, 34A, 41A, 43A, and 22D are really not worthy of a Saturday puzzle. Long, easy-to-figure out fill like this makes the overall puzzle much easier. The other long spanners were equally obvious with one or two cross letters. The clueing was also pretty straight forward for the most part. Days past (yeah, I'm old), I'd be chewing on a Saturday puzzle off and on throughout the day, and maybe into Sunday (or beyond!).

I got side-tracked reading comments...  
This was a weird-ish puzzle for me.  A couple of spots gave me pause (as in, "Eww," or "Wha'?")  

FRIED EGGS are not ordered "over well;" please come up with something better.  No clue at all about LIM as a "Calc. Calculation" or the Gardener person OR the Miami Heat, but even so....
any of those were preferable to 21A, where I was expecting SEXY TALK or somethiing like that...even though it was a break from the pedestrian I'M FOR IT MADE IT WE HAD A DEAL.  
If you need a SAFE WORD in the BR,  aren't you trusting the wrong person.  Shouldn't "Stop" do?

I wanted the "Cowboy's friend" to be his HOSS.  You'd have to dig up old John Wayne Westerns to unearth PARD (short for PARTNER, pronounced in folksy fashion) and anyway he preferred "Pilgrim"  pronounced "Pil-grum."

18A is just plain incorrect.

A MOD is any third-party modification to a game. It *could* be a new outfit or accessory, but it could just as easily be an expanded map, an additional character, a cheat, a bug fix, or any number of things. Additionally, obtaining a new outfit or accessory doesn't necessarily require a MOD. Many games revolve around finding higher-level gear as you progress. A MOD might add extra outfits, but the vanilla game usually has a ton of those already.

The clue just fails on every level. It's awoken my nerd rage.

Took me a second or two running through the first clues but once I got my first answer it flowed pretty easily. Actually found it less difficult than expected for Saturday. Answers I might have struggled with were fairly easy because of the crosses.  Never thought i’d finish so quickly

I know I'm always bringing up the rear, but still a bit surprised that so many found this unusually easy for a Saturday. Typical long workout for me, and did have to look up a couple of things. No big deal - that's just me. One puzzle find today - a rather amazing Sunday from March 9, 1986 by Joy L. Wouk with the title: "Beastly Combos." Some clue/answer examples: "Horticultural pachyderm" RHINOCEROSEFANCIER "Dabbling reptile." CROCODILETTANTE And some other theme answers: LIONESSAYIST GORILLABORER ELEPHANTOMS HOUNDERLING HIPPOPOTAMUSKETEER Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=3/9/1986&g=62&d=A Might put another puzzle find in a reply. ....

Loved it!  Especially loved the stack of CROCODILE TEARS and ROOKIE MISTAKE, but all the fill is colorful and interesting.  And there's almost no crosswordese.

How ambiguous the phrase "In time" is.  it can mean in the nick of time, as in at the eleventh hour, or it can mean don't wait up for it, it will come whenever it bloody well wants to come.  As in SOONER OR LATER.  I was initially looking for the first meaning, so SOONER OR LATER took me a while to get -- even when I had SOON.  I really wanted SOON ENOUGH.

SAFE WORD in the bedroom?  What's that?  Safe from whom?  Does it have something to do with sex?  Or does it have something to do with sanctuary?

A very enjoyable Saturday with a lovely grid.  I have to jog my memory as to Mon and Tues and Wed of this week since I don't tend to remember puzzles, but I do remember this past Sunday, Thursday and Friday.  I loved them all.  This may have been that rarest thing of all:  a Perfect Puzzle Week!

Hi, Jesse! As a new solver (only solving for a couple of years), I liked your puzzle. I could tell right away that was a hard puzzle to create and I salute you for your perseverance! I loved the long solves; they were somewhat easy for me and helped me to get the rest of the puzzle. I  do not care if a puzzle is a “Friday” or “Tuesday;” I just love a challenging solve. I will be watching for your next offering.

"Neon (Ne) is the fifth most abundant element in the universe, following hydrogen, helium, oxygen, and carbon. 

It is a noble gas created as an intermediate product during the fusion process in massive stars, just before they go supernova. 

Neon accounts for roughly 0.1% of the elemental mass in the cosmos"

Always loved the term "Noble Gas".
That's not what a MOD is. That is all.

SUNDAY WEEKEND EDITION SOLVERS -- DO NOT SOLVE THE 4/19/26 Crossword in the Magazine today!  I wracked my brain for 15 minutes, and ran to reddit to confirm THERE IS AN ERROR WITH THE GRID!!!!  Correct one will be in the news section tomorrow.  GRRRRR!

Slightly chewy but not much of a challenge in the end.  XWStats is showing "Easy" although I might have said moderate or something.  Nice to see respect to Martin Gardner.

I've not stopped by the comments much lately since every puzzle has become "unremarkable".  Perhaps about 10-15% due to my increasing familiarity and 85% to lowered standards.  Thursdays might as well not exist.  :(

I do miss the cleverness, bite, and incisiveness of posters such as Andrzej and Lewis and Munster Mike and SP and Eric H. and others.  But what is there to talk about, really?

In the last week I've caught up on New Yorker crosswords since the new year.  Turns out our esteemed Robyn Weintraub appears there almost weekly.  With that and LA Times, the NYT is becoming just another site visit for me.

I know, I know, don't let the door hit me on the way out.  I'll probably stop by for interesting puzzles.  Enjoy, all, and it's been lovely talking to you.  :)
I used to love the imagination and humor of Joel Fagliano's Mini puzzles. But that all disappeared a year or so ago.  Today's mini has so.much.trivia-- I think, Joel ole buddy: wha' happened?

Great puzzle overall!  Took me a good while to finish and a lot of head scratching without ever leaving me feeling completely stumped. So many answers I struggled to find seemed so obvious once I got them, a wonderful feeling!

Clever.   Surprising at times.  Interesting.  Satisfying!

Enjoyed it!

I just finished reading “The Whys of a Philosophical Scrivener” by Martin Gardner and I was long a fan of his Mathematical Games column so I was glad to see a reference to him!  

Was that his first mention in the crossword?
Thawing. . . . . Detente?. . . .huh? Imprecise cluing. And aren't the parts of speech in clue and answer supposed to agree?
Fun puzzle! Really enjoyed some of the answers (crocodile tears)
That. Was. Awesome.

Did you know that "RABBIT MISTAKE" and "ROOKIE MISTAKE" have the same amount of letters? 

You know, like when you confuse two rabbits. Classic vet error and perfectly logical entry.
Wow. I was *this close* to just googling through it but that would have been a ROOKIEMISTAKE. Little slower than my average but very satisfying.
Today I was taught to realize that KNIGHTS, DWARVES, MAIDENS, and DAMSELS, unfortunately in that order, all have the same number of letters.

When I had no fill for the first nine clues I got excited, thinking this one would take awhile. When I finished at close to my personal best I felt a bit of whiplash and was tempted to post that it was easy. Fortunately, I've learned the hard way that Very Hards can seem easy and Averages can seem hard to me. My sample of one isn't very informative.

Leaving me to wonder whether the puzzle was easy for most solvers or was just in my personal wheelhouse.

Today I think it was both. Easy because 55% of the (expert) solvers who report on XWInfo solved much faster than their recent Saturday averages, but also in my wheelhouse, because my solve time is usually way, way longer than the median times reported by those experts, and today it wasn't. 

For those who found it hard, realize that 18% of that tough crowd solved it slower than their Saturday average. So you are in good company! And hey, I really did enjoy this puzzle and laughed a lot at the answers.
Trying to explain SAFEWORD to a juvenile solver. Got a cluefor that?

Should SAFEWORD be here? I'm not offended by the idea, but isn't the fun of such things that they are for use in a specific context? Much as I love it, I struggle to think of anything less erotic than the NYT crossword.

Living in close proximity to the Chesapeake Bay, I'm not a fan of anything that contains "imitation crab meat." We prefer the genuine article. With that said, CALIFORNIA ROLL was a lovely long entry, as was CROCODILE TEARS. Nicely clued, as well. No prob with PARD, but the clue for FRIED EGGS was a bit of a stretch. Aren't they over hard?
As someone with a b.a. in international relations, I'm embarrassed with how long it took me to get 16A...

Well, it was bound to be done SOONERORLATER...  For me today, it was very much sooner. 

It was a fun puzzle, but I am kind of weirded out by how the last two puzzles have exceeded my personal bests by kind of crazy percentages. I don't aim to be a speed solver but today and yesterday I found myself practically done before I started. I'm really not that good.  Ah well!  

Anyhow, my family of origin was very much a HASSOCK family, but I felt like no one else in my life ever knew what that word meant. Everyone outside of my family calls it an ottoman. So it was fun to have some validation that my mom didn't make up that word. Or else, she made it up and it finally took. Who's to say!? Probably a dictionary, but who's got time for that!? Har!

Another family thing, each and every one of us from our family of origin are eggs over medium with extra crispy corned beef hash. It's rare that we would all be together for breakfast these days, but when we used to, even as adults, we would go around the table and each one say the exact same order. My siblings spouses have also succumbed. My man will never! I'm strangely proud of him for it, though he is wrong. 

Speaking of eggs, I saw on TV an Italian chef character making eggs basically poached in tomato sauce. We were fascinated! I can't remember off hand the Italian name, but I've seen it referred as eggs in purgatory and we are going to have to give it a try soon. 

If anyone makes this, I'm happy for any tips. Bill in Detroit??

I enjoyed the puzzle, but I'm feeling a bit frustrated with the games department. First of all, you may have seen the editor's note that there's an error in the print version of Sundays puzzle in the magazine. That's the second time this week that something went to press with errors. Then when I went to do the Midi I had to do that ridiculous dance where I get the error message that the page no longer exists and I have to keep refreshing until the puzzle magically appears. I can't believe the software is still glitching so long after the Midi's introduction. Finally, my solve time on the main puzzle was respectable--faster than my average by a few minutes. But when I went to look at the Statistics page it listed my solve time as ten minutes faster than it shows on the actual puzzle timer. And when I refreshed the puzzle tab, the new/faster/incorrect time showed up.

I know there are a lot of moving parts, but it really seems as if things are going off the rails. Sorry to be a downer, I'm just as I said frustrated. And a little sad.

Wow.  Finished in 60% less time than my Saturday average.  Still it did feel harder than a Wednesday even though it was very close, but slightly higher, than my Wednesday average.  I enjoy a challenge, but it's nice to sail through one now and then.

I thought of SPINOFF early on for 1A, but hesitated to fill it in until the crosses made it seem inevitable, because I did not think the Simpsons was a spinoff.  Afterward I discovered why:  I did not watch the Tracy Ullman show.
MOD for 18A is silly-- never heard this said, ever
Back in my drinking days, I used to get SOUSED on the SAUCE.
If we disallowed humblebrags about people’s solve times, what’s the over/under on the number of comments we’d lose? A third? Half?
I enjoyed the very clean fill on this Saturday puzzle. Hats off to Jesse.
I finished but scratching my head over answers to treat and mouseketeers tool and a few others

I always appreciate a grid that goes from achingly empty after browsing the Acrosses to suddenly, smoothly filled — though admittedly this one was a bit more blink-of-an-eye than my typical Saturday solve. 

COW CORN before PET FOOD at 29D... Anyone else?? Haha not really, entirely, because I did have the P already. But it fit, and would've been sooo gooood. Also had an assortment of drunken synonyms before landing on SAUCED, partly because I was half convinced [New York's ___ Valley] just had to be something much more obscure than HUDSON-! Rather on the nose, but oh well. An enjoyable romp rather than a cliff hike is just as good for the soul. Thanks Jesse!
Initially slow, but then finished about 15 min. faster than usual. 🐊💧💧
A satisfying solve. Once I got CALIFORNIAROLL I was off and running. I thought a cowboy’s friend might be his “hoss,” but PARD is probably better, at least for most cowboys.
Mods are client side changes to a program almost always made by fans or users. For games it is uncommon for them to be cosmetic only, and rarely official as suggested by 18A.
Hard to finish the puzzle when you have cassock instead of hassock. Even so managed to figure in slightly less than average. A fine puzzle

Tough, but satisfying.

The misdirects were generally quite good.

One more late puzzle find. This one I found after it dawned on me that.... BIRDSOFAFEATHER is 15 letters. And.. yep, it's been an answer in 23 puzzles, often part of the theme in various ways. But one in particular really struck me - a Thursday from July 31, 1986 by Shirley Soloway. Three other 15 letter answers in that one: TOOLSOFTHETRADE MIDDLEOFNOWHERE SIGNSOFTHETIMES Thought that was pretty impressive. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=7/31/1986&g=65&d=A It's late, so I might re-post this tomorrow. ...
I did not enjoy this puzzle, not even one bit.

Very easy for a Saturday or maybe I’m getting better????
Nah.

When I was a teen (or maybe younger) a dentist accused me of shedding CROCODILETEARS. At first I didn't know what he meant; after it was explained I was offended that he would think he could know what pain I might be experiencing. That phrase has stuck with me as a result! (and was a gimme today)
Bombardiro Crocodilo

I had never heard of Dwade - I had emits instead of edits. What a cool nickname! Dwayne Wade to Dwade, Maybe I’ll remember he played for the Heat because of Dade county.

Obligatory “this was too easy” - but some fun clues. I liked rookie mistakes, and it was interesting to see all the many F squares. I wish there had been fewer food clues - it’s bedtime here not lunch time 😂

Hope everyone is having a nice weekend. 

What was that surfer slang word we had a few weeks ago? Anyone remember? I’m on a mission to remember more trivia.
13;25 nice puzzle for sure

This was weird. I really did not enjoy the puzzle, but I feel like I'd get along well with it's creator.
Too many clues seemed like a stretch. Some were really fun though

Francis,
I don't make stone tablets; The Times delivers them to my cave.

@Barry Ancona
I’m typing this with a stylus on an Apple tablet.

@Barry Ancona

You get home delivery by puzzle time?  I’m impressed.
Something's definitely amiss when I finish faster than the xwstats Median Solve Time (as I did today)

@Barry Ancona

Certainly more than half of the coffee should be missing from the first cup before the puzzle is complete.
@kkseattle Seriously. Weekend swords used to be an activity. Now each one is just a quick <10 minute blip in the day.
@Barry Ancona  Agreed. My fastest Saturday (almost 20 minutes below my average Saturday!)

@Francis

I have done these puzzles under a considerable variety of influences. Happy to exchange notes anytime.
@Mike If you need a snappy response, I’m sure someone will bite! I’m just not sure if i m-on-it-or someone else is… anyone else want to dive in?

@Mike
Some day Iguana post before you do.  Then I'll be ahead from the gecko.

@Mike
I caiman thinking I had a pun…oh, well.

@Mean Old Lady
Cute 👍

@Matt

It was a lovely Wednesday puzzle.

@Matt saw MICRO, RAMROD, IMFORIT and his brain lit up the EEG.

{BAZINGA}

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Becky

Weak tea, Becky.

Francis,
My father didn't do the crossword, but my (maternal) grandmother did, and she would have gotten a kick out of 21A.

@Francis

Ðęàðáşś!!!

@Barry Ancona

Good for her!

@Francis

The day I knew my blessed grandmother was no longer with me in spirit in completing the puzzle (she had left me in body some years earlier) was the day FO SHIZZLE appeared (April 18! 2014).

@Sue I have also started doing this, but find some of them difficult. The name that was so familiar now eludes me. But I do like coming upon words that I would swear I never heard of much less remember them being used. It’s great to have access to those puzzles and sometimes it can be a challenge which is fun. They often “reach back” to stars and public figures from the ‘30s forward. Although that was not my generation, I realize that I have absorbed that history.

@Sue, that’s so interesting - I’m a relatively new solver (a couple of years in) and have been delving into the archives. I often find the older puzzles harder, but I’ve also wondered how much of that is the lack of familiarity with the contemporary references. And my fastest ever time was on a Monday crossword from 2008!

@Sue

There are definitely some puzzles from back in the day that rely on trivia to an extent that the editors (to their credit, IMO) wouldn’t let through today. And if you had unusually strong knowledge of trivia, I can easily see how you’d have an easier time with such puzzles than most people.

But on the other hand…there are several regular comments in this forum who have been doing the puzzle for *decades* and feel very strongly that there has been a marked decline in difficulty in recent years, and that the change hasn’t been subtle.

As someone who has spent lots of time in the archives, I agree with them completely. In my own judgment, a fundamental change that has taken place within the last few years: in contrast to “the good old days”, the editors are no longer willing (except perhaps on increasingly rare occasions) to publish puzzles that seem likely to stump a significant proportion of the solving population. That, and they seem to have concluded that the average Saturday should be approachable to the average solver.

@Mark

A big part of what makes the before-and-after so stark to me is that I tend to do at least one or two archive puzzles each day, and the Saturday puzzles from as recently as April 2012 (the month I currently happen to be visiting in the archives) have all been *much* harder for me than any puzzle in recent memory.

Another thing that stands out from the archives is how they used to throw in the occasional Wednesday that was legitimately challenging — heck, even the odd Tuesday would have a handful of seriously tough entries.

Point being: given that I was already an adult with a fully developed frontal lobe in 2012, Sue’s hypothesis (that puzzles nowadays just *seem* easier because they feature trivia that’s in the wheelhouse of younger generations) doesn’t ring true to me at all.

@Anonymous
It's probably too late for you to see this, and I agree with much of what you say, but as to you having more difficulty with puzzles from 14 years ago than current one, I think you'd likely acknowledge that a certain percentage of clues are always going to be more accessible to the synapses in real rather than historical time.
@Dave K. RESTEASY, the president is a DOCTOR

@Aaron
We need a laugh emoji. On second thought, make that a cry emoji.

@Aaron

Jesus, that made me laugh!
@ad absurdum BOOM!
@Aaron Yes… Doctor Demento. (Apologies to a brilliant radio artist.)

@Dave K.
the MICROphallus at DoD
when not SAUCED on Texas tea
knows ROI not ROE
and does LAT pulldowns on TV
@JS brilliant..

@Anita

Ditto on DOG FOOD, ROOKIE MISTAKE and HASSOCKS.
@Anita I'm embarrassed to say I started with "pig food". Prodigal son on the brain?

@Steven M.
I was one of those strange people who used to love driving in Manhattan, but freaked out on wide open spaces like I-70 from the Denver airport to Breckenridge and those other towns.

@Times Rita
I've never understood why driving in Manhattan gets such a bad rap. I get that it can be quite busy and noisy, but I'll take driving on a grid any day over the haphazard, apparently-laid-out-during-a-hangover mazes that you find in a lot of major cities. I visited my niece several years ago while she was there for ten months working in a high-level job on the set of Mission Impossible. They rented her a nice house and a car, and on the first day she tried driving from the house to the set, she ended up pulling over in tears. And she's a good driver, having lived in Minneapolis, Los Angeles, and Nashville.

@Bob T.
Love that, I'll get some good mileage from it! Thanks.

@Lauren

MICRO is a prefix in metric measurement; macro is not. Now if the clue were about prefixes for economics, you could argue two correct possibilities.

@john ezra
Thanks for the much needed laugh, John!  You really need a warning on this post:  “Do not read with a mouthful of wine!”
@The X-Phile, you mean the Dallas-Fort Worth airport is not family friendly? Good to know.

@Francis if you were Be8 you’d be a bottleneck in nucleosynthesis and making the sun glow. But if you were the doubly magic Ni56 (also all alphas) you’d also decay (somewhat slower 7.7 days) but you’d be powering Supernovae and producing almost all the iron in the universe.

@Ιασων

Thank you!  I was hoping for exactly that kind of reply.  

I'm aware that Be-8 does hang together just long enough for third alpha to collide to produce C-12.  Is that the bottleneck you are referring to?

And yes, the "multiple alpha particle" thing falls apart, it's a nice trend for a while (excluding that stupid Be-8).

@Francis

If only more comments were like yours, full of interesting facts and offbeat takes rather than the all-too-common “too easy” or “too naticky” complaints. You are NEON, my friend. Independent, hard to pin down, and rarely found on Earth. Thanks for making the comment section glow.

@Francis

Wow, I blew the half life stuff.  It should only be Be-8... there is not Be-14.  I don't know where that came from.

@Heidi

Aw, thanks.  You're very nice, I think, although it's possible to interpret "[you're] rarely found on Earth" in both a positive and a negative sense :-)

@Francis

I knew you’d deflect. You know what I meant. 🙂

Hi, @Francis! How long have you been commenting on this forum? (How long have I?) You have certain become a vital, integral member of this community!
Thank you!

@Francis
To continue--as one who has an amateur's interest in, and knowledge of, the physical sciences, but who also has Zeno of Elea over regularly for dinner (tortoise soup) and a game of darts, there's one thing about nuclear decay that I've always wondered.

I understand that if an isotope has a half-life of x minutes, in x minutes you'd have half as much of it; after 2x minutes only 1/4 as much; after 3x minutes only 1/8. But at some point, you'll have only one atom--what then? It is either all, or nothing.

Or am I overthinking this?

I, for one, am glad that you are *not* a Be8 nucleus!

@Francis
Again, a great Tiny Desk performance! Now I'll need to research how neon is extracted.

@Heidi

Ok, but…today was way too easy for a Saturday, right?

@Francis indeed that is the bottleneck. You can’t get from He to C because Be is poison. It takes you back to He. But thanks to Fred Hoyle we have a way out. 

As I’m sure you know Neon not only has a stable nucleus but also closed shells of electrons. It really doesn’t care for any kind of interaction.

@Bill IIRC, at the level of individual atoms, half-life is actually a probability function over time: for a half life of X (nanoseconds to millions of years) any given atom has a 50% chance of decaying, not that exactly 50% of the atoms will decay, so that last one would eventually decay as well.

It's like precipitation probability, where a 70% chance of rain means any given location in the forecast area has a 70% chance of getting rained on, not that 70% of the forecast area will get rained on.

@Bill
Bill, I LOVE questions like this. Hope you get an answer from Francis, who is to be commended for her very interesting and diverting "comment."

@Francis
You know why your first reply got held up by an emu, right? It wanted to make sure your insult to Be-8 posted more than 80 billionths of a billionth of a second later.

@Mark

Francis with an I. Always the male version of the name, and this Francis is, indeed, a man. (Frances with an E is female – like my middle name.)

Not sure why you put "comment" in quotes?

And I agree: conversations like these are always the very best part of these comments – thanks all!
@Francis thank you for sharing this!

@Mark

Ah, that makes sense about "comment"! Occasionally I'll use the word "post", especially in reference to an initial, well, comment. Thanks for coming back to explain.

@Bill

Ed H. said it better than I could. But I'll try to support it.

All along, simply watching one atom would be just like watching the "last" atom.  When the number of remaining atoms is large, we can make predictions about the sample as a whole.

That all ends when the sample gets small.  An remember we're comparing small to billions of billions.

I like saying "billions".  I reminds me of Carl Sagan, who died way, way, way too young.

@Nora

Iron would have been a good guess.  And it was my first one.

The reason there's so much iron is that the iron-56 nuclide has the greatest binding energy (energy it takes to "unbind" the nucleons) per nucleon of any nuclide anywhere.  Making any nucleus larger than iron requires the input of energy.

So iron is a kind of dead end, unless there are a whole lot of neutrons around, which is how the larger elements get built.  And the average star doesn't have an especially high neutron density.

But, boy, those novas and supernovas...neutrons galore.

I love writing about this stuff.  I'm glad I've found people who can tolerate it.

@Mark

No offense taken at all.  I'm glad you posted your comment!
@Francis And of course neon has the stable octet of electrons which renders it largely inert.

@Heidi,

Unlike NEON, however, @Francis does have an affinity for interaction. 🙂

@Francis
In case you come back to this, Francis, my bigger gaffe was not commenting on your Grand Marais locale, what with me having spent many summer vacations at Lutsen Lodge and my dad and brother and his family living a little ways to your west, on the Iron Range.

@Marshall Walthew

Could have written this comment myself. Had the same experience, almost nothing until CROCODILETEARS and then the flood; and also great memories of MARTIN Gardner

@Marshall Walthew

I'm a MARTIN Gardner fan, too.
And his name just came up in conversation with respect to my spouse's reading a tome by Douglas Hofstadter.  Gardner's column in the "Scientific American", "Mathematical Games", became Hofstadter's "Metamagical Themas".
I'm sure you noticed that Hofstadter's title is an angram of Gardner's.  
(He's such a show-off!)

@Francis

So, did I or didn’t I?

@Marshall Walthew

You did.  Congratulations!  🥳

@Chris

ARLES, France, is a location where Van Gogh lived and did a lot of his painting, including Starry Night. DETENTE is an easing of tension between two opposing factions or countries. Something like "we might not be friends, but we won't try to kill each other right now". PARD is a slangy abbreviation of "partner". HASSOCK is another name for ottoman and is an upholstered footstool.
@JayTee - Just to add we had a lot of talk of nuclear detente with the USSR under Nixon and Ford.
@Chris Well, now you know both ARLES and DETENTE. The former is quite common here and the latter shows up periodically.

@Nora
Why dubious about PARD? Long before I read any of the Bard, I'd seen plenty of horse operas in which one cowboy called another PARD,  as in "Put 'em up, pard!"

@SP
Oh and I did really enjoy ROOKIEMISTAKE as well.

commentBody:
@Andy G

Welcome to commenting on the forum!

I believe we are an affable group, at least when we're not ripping each other to shreds.  Just tread lightly on full moons.

The "M" in MOD was the last thing I got in.

@Andy G

I’m not a gamer, so I will defer to your take while admitting I had no idea how to fill those three squares. I got the answer solely through the crosses. I like your alternate clue, though— I’d have gotten that one!

@Andy G
I was thinking skin.  But it didn’t fit. 

But didn’t you answer your own comment regarding modification (mod).  Like how you can mod weapons in Fortnite.  

Sky River!

@Andy G agreed!  My friends and I are pretty big gamers. Mods are a significantly broader category than merely cosmetics (as specified in the clue) to the point that for some games, cosmetics are even considered to be their own thing, not mods at all (eg. “custom content” in Sims 4). I penciled in the right answer, but thought it was probably wrong until I literally finished the whole puzzle because of how poorly it fit the clue. 

I’m guessing the puzzle maker and editor aren’t gamers, which is how they ended up on this clue. Speaking as someone who is though, I’d suggest MODifying it. Perhaps “new move or accessory…” or “new map…” to stick with the video game theme.

Maggie,
You may be in the minority, but you are not alone.

Global Stats
Difficulty  Easy
Median Solve Time  13:55
Median Solver  25% faster
⚡84% of users solved faster than their Saturday average.
58% solved much faster (>20%) than their Saturday average.
🐢16% of users solved slower than their Saturday average.
5% solved much slower (>20%) than their Saturday average.

@Barry Ancona Xstats are suspect.  It's a self selecting group of presumably strong solvers.  Possibly offsetting that is the fact that people sometimes wander away as the clock runs.  And offsetting that is the probability that some people cheat to lower their times.  Where does that leave us?
@Heidi interesting, I found that to be not only the most clever clue in this puzzle, but in any of Phil Collins’ NYT crossword puzzles.

@lucky13 absolutely. The Hudson Valley School is considered one of the pre-eminent art movements of the early twentieth century. Personal favorites include Grundle McFaden, Smooth Sack Rogers, and Ilsa “Rubber Thigh” von Holnbrecht.  One thing that always strikes me when looking at work from these singular artists was their commitment to using only spaghetti sauce and glue as their media of choice.

@Ace

I feel like I'm sitting next to Cliff Clavin at Cheers.

@lucky13
I multi-task a phone game with NYTimes games, often changing my avatar’s outfits, never heard of COS, got it after trying MOD, thought it might refer to COSPLAY which I know very little about.  This isn’t the first gamer word I learned in the crosswords.

@Francis
Same solution as on Cheers: change barstools or, in chronic cases, bars.
@Weak yeah it’s in reference to that time Bruce Vilanch hid out in a coffee cup for over four hours straight in order to surprise me and Elaine Stritch.

@Ace
Just read a fascinating Wikipedia article about north sentinel island, which I had never heard of (of which I had never heard?)
So thanks for that.
And sorry about Bruce ruining the cappuccino.
@Rahul, be glad you don't know. I'm glad I don't know from experience.

@Rahul
I had to look it up. I'm certainly no pearl-clutcher, but now the crossword has descended into BDSM?
50 shades of the Overton window

@Rahul

A SAFE WORD can be used during sex to indicate that you don't feel comfortable with what's going on.  It's mostly associated with BDSM.
@Asher What got you?

@Jake G 
a lot of things: Spinoff, doctor, safeword (what is that?), dwade (who is that?) pard, lat, it just didn't click for me. Im not complaining, it happens.

@Bill
I thought SIX wd take 2 hands, myself....
@Lewis I always appreciate your positivity.  Thanks!

@Lewis
Just out of curiosity, is there anything to WREAK other than havoc and maybe chaos? One doesn't WREAK iron, though it can certainly be wrought.
@Timesnlatte Agreed.  I expect to savor my Saturday solve for more than 15 short minutes.  What should I do with the rest of my morning?
It is, but some things are even better when they last longer...

@Pam C.
Should be pinned to the top of the breaking news page, no question.
Shocking indeed!
😁😁

Pam C.,
I'm going to try to solve it!

@Barry Ancona 

Just use the Hal in Long Island method. Still miss his comments.

Vaer,
Looked at it. 
Solve not happening (except by Hal).
Never got to meet him at Tracks.

@Pam C.
I am so looking forward to this Sunday NYT Crossword in the actual newspaper. I always preferred to solve the physical newspaper puzzle (in pencil) but the NYT ended carrier delivery here (except on Sundays) a couple of years ago, so I do the crossword on line Monday through Saturday.   Sundays I print the newspaper version because I do not like to do the crossword on the shiny magazine paper. So, yay, tomorrow, a newsprint Sunday NYT crossword puzzle!

Vaer,
For Magazine solvers who don't want to wait until tomorrow morning for the "daily" Sunday paper for the corrected puzzle, I hear the Newspaper Version PDF (available at 6 p.m. today) will be the correct one ... albeit smaller than the actual print one.

@Esmerelda
They have been collected in book form and are still in print, I believe.

Joe,
Wanting RApier before RAMROD was my only slowdown.

@Ιασων BAMA is one of the most famous college football teams here, consistently vying for the national title and possibly better-known than many NFL teams in the general population. Blitzed is definitely a slang synonym for drunk here. PARD is absolutely a word in the context of westerns (movies). “Over well” is certainly a way to describe how you want your eggs cooked in the local greasy spoon diner. So yes, harder for non-Americans, but still very much in the vernacular of American constructors, editors and solvers.

@Tim

I like your P --> W swap. But not your clue for WARD. You could use "Protectee" I suppose, or refer to a wing in an institution, or even clue WARD as a verb, with (off). WETFOOD seems fine to me, as a response to your clue.

@Xword Junkie
"Chemically inert and volatile"?! Is this another Al hallucination? Don't people read this stuff before they cut-and-paste? Help me out, please.

@Jennifer it seems like as one gets older, crosswords should get easier as one has had longer to learn and experience more. Not just in life, but also in crosswords. Perhaps older clues seem harder now as they become less topical and more archaic. Or maybe they really are getting easier in general. Probably a bit of both, IMO.

@Mean Old Lady I agree with your general assessment of this puzzle, but I read the FRIED EGGS clue as it was probably intended. I.e., "well" modifies "going over" rather than the resulting condition of the eggs. Well done eggs are just gross.
@Mean Old Lady LIM refers to limit, a concept in calculus (i.e. Calc.)
@Mean Old Lady Uh, no. A safe word is needed because, sometimes, between two people, when they feel comfortable with each other, “stop“ doesn’t mean stop. Clear?

@Mean Old Lady - You may not ever order eggs "over well" and I may not ever order my eggs "over well," but there are people out there who do. I have a friend who does this. He also orders his steaks very well done. Paradoxically, he considers himself a bit of a gourmet and an arbiter of taste when it comes to cuisine. 

I have told him many times I cannot trust the culinary judgment of anyone who orders a steak well done. He is immutable on this front. About his eggs, too. 

Interesting (perhaps?) aside: my father loved his fried eggs crispy but with a runny yolk. He cooked them in a small cast-iron pan reserved for his eggs only. He would heat the pan searingly hot, add butter, and then crack an egg into it. It would be flipped briefly at one point -- I guess over easy -- and then onto the plate it would go. Browned, crispy white around the edges, but a perfectly runny yolk in the middle. 

I have yet to be able to reproduce his fried eggs. I wish he were still around to teach me.

@MOL et al. For all those throwin' shade at fried eggs "over well": Undercooked eggs--such as "over easy" or soft-boiled--carry a potential risk of salmonella--about 1 in 10 000 are contaminated, a county health inspector once told me. Which isn't going to stop me eating them. But my eyes are open to the risk. FWIW, most food service establishments will buy pasteurized yolks, and whites, in cartons, which can be used in instances where raw eggs are traditionally used: mousses, and emulsified dressings and sauces. The pasteurized whites are totally useless for meringues, etc., IMO. Pasteurized shell eggs are also available, but they're very expensive, and somewhat creepy. https://www.foodsafety.gov/blog/salmonella-and-eggs

@Kirsten
I do not recall seeing it, but Soph year was long, long ago, and I definitely did not get into advanced mathematics as a special ed teacher.    Abbreviating something that short seems odd.
@Bill 😂😂😂 “cacao” is great

@Bill
“Ramrod!” may be an equally bad choice.
@Katie If a new outfit or accessory is one example of a MOD, then the clue is correct.

@Lin it’s like if “rainy weeks” was used to clue SUMMER. It’s not technically wrong because there are rainy weeks in summer, but it’s both a really bad example of summer weather and an accurate description of other times of year. 

Mods as a category don’t always include cosmetics and many cosmetics are acquired in the course of normal gameplay. The issue isn’t that the clue is overly specific, but that it uses such bad examples that the answer no longer fits.

@Lin 
Neither a new outfit nor an accessory are examples of mods.

@Rich in Atlanta As threatened. A Tuesday from July 26, 2016 by Bruce Haight. Some asterisked answers, all straightforwardly clued and all crossing at least one other one across and down. SIDETABLE SNEAKTHIEF SITTIGHT SONGTITLE SEATRIP SORETHUMBS SWEETTOOTH SADTALE SURETHING SOULTRAIN STARTREK And there were more. And then the 'reveal' "Girl's name that phonetically provides the initials to the answers to the asterisked clues." ESTEE Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=7/26/2016&g=58&d=A ....

@Rich in Atlanta

CROCODILETTANTE is awesome. And it's amazing that that crossword had not one but two answers that relate to today's puzzle! Very cool.

@Nancy
Your safe word may be Diebold or Mosler, if your safe is in the bedroom. 😋
@Jerry or a Chubb
@Nancy If your question is an earnest one, a safeword is an agreed upon codeword used to revoke consent or otherwise indicate your desire to stop without explicitly saying so
@Nancy Dictionary for SAFEWORD?

@lioncitysolver

If you’ve got a Chubb, she’s never safe.

@Nancy 
Hope you saw the comments or editor's note stating that the Sunday puzzle in the Magazine is wrong. Correct version to be published in main section tomorrow.

@Stocc -- It was indeed an earnest question -- though I'm now feeling like an idiot for not knowing or remembering that.  I'm sure I've read about it more than once -- many times, most likely.  I probably blocked it out unconsciously because I found the whole idea so disquieting.
@lioncitysolver bwa ha ha
@Vaer -- I didn't see it!!!  Thanks!!!!
@katie The other day they described Sum41 as "skatepunk."  Clues are getting a little dicey.
@katie Absolutely. A MOD is an additional program, the clue is describing a Skin, and they're not the same.

@Raliegh
100%. I read the clue and thought — that’s a skin. There’s no other gamer term for it. Bad clue. Mods are completely different.
@katie Couldn't agree more and ran to the comments to note the same thing!
@katie  I'm concerned about the grandmas and grandpas who try to earn cool points with their grandkids this weekend and end up embarrassing themselves.

@katie its a fair clue. In some games, like Cyberpunk 2077 (which I'm currently playing) you can add attachments and mods to weapons, for example. https://cyberpunk.fandom.com/wiki/Cyberpunk_2077_Attachments_and_Modifications#Modifications

@jf

It certainly made answering that clue a lot tougher! Ditto MOD, though I had the M and had a feeling that was going to be the answer. 🙃
@MFSTEVE not an outfit or an attachment for a character.
@Dawn This mistake is also clearly identified with an apology in the Wordplay article that you are commenting on.

@Dawn

Thank you for the post.  I did the same and then came here to see if there was an explanation.

Greg,
Did you also come to the Wordplay comments 
before looking at the Wordplay column?
@Barry Ancona No crime in that!!

@B

Always good to see your byline here.  Even when we've disagreed, I've enjoyed the sparring.

Here are a few pertinent  proverbs:

"The fruit of silence is tranquillity."

Proverbs 17:27
He who restrains his words has knowledge,
And he who has a cool spirit is a man of understanding.

When the eagles are silent, the parrots begin to jabber.
Winston Churchill
@Wendy P Me too!!

@Wendy P

That’s what she said 😉

@Anonymous

Just before she used the safe word.
@Gregory Melahn  Martin Gardner seems pretty durned obscure to any reader who is not a mathematician--say, 98 percent of Times readers?

@Gregory Melahn 
I had never heard of him. I tried Melvin, then MARTha, then landed it with MARTIN.

@Gregory Melahn - I always opened Gardner's column first when Scientific American arrived. This NON-MATHEMATICIAN  loved the column (which was written for the general educated reader, not MATHEMATICIANS) as well as its successor, Metamagical Themas. 

The target audience of these columns were not mathematicians, but curious readers -- and I suspect anyone who is serious about NYT puzzles would also love his MATHEMATICAL GAMES column. And Metamagical Themas.

This is a Times debut for MARTIN clued to Gardner.
GARDNER was clued once to Martin.
